findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

findings.expected: permissions_boundary is the maximum-permissions ceiling on a role. Without it, a permissive policy attached later can grant unintended privileges. Roles created by developers should ALWAYS have a boundary set.
findings.message: IAM role lambda: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
remediation: Define an aws_iam_policy that represents the maximum allowed permissions for this role's category (e.g. "developer-boundary"). Set permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn on the role.
findings.suggestion: permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn
findings.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.message: results: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function processor: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function processor: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda processor: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-throttles"

  statistic           = "Sum"

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda processor: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy lambda_kms: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Sid = "UsePipelineKey", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"], Resource = aws_kms_key.pipeline.arn }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with expiration and abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rules
findings.message: S3 bucket (logs) has no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.
remediation: Add aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with an abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rule (7 days) to clean up abandoned uploads — these are invisible in the console but accumulate.
Add expiration rules for objects older than your retention window. For versioned buckets, add noncurrent_version_expiration (e.g. 30 days) to prevent version tombstones from accumulating.
findings.title: 1 S3 bucket without a lifecycle configuration

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: default_tags { tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } }
findings.message: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
remediation: Add default_tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } to the provider block. New resources inherit automatically.
findings.title: AWS provider block has no default_tags

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.processor and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_lambda_function.processor is not invoked async-push (sync via API GW / Function URL, or polled-async via SQS / Kinesis event source mapping) — Lambda-level DLQ would never receive events. The invoker handles failures.
context_suppressed.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

findings.expected: A sibling aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation resource, or a rotation lambda attached
findings.message: Secrets Manager secret redshift: verify a rotation schedule is configured (aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation with rotation_rules.automatically_after_days <= 90).
remediation: Add an aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation referencing the secret, with automatically_after_days = 30 or 90
Implement the rotation Lambda (AWS publishes templates for RDS, DocumentDB, Redshift, and custom use cases)
findings.title: Secrets Manager secret should have automatic rotation configured

findings.expected: parameter { name = "require_ssl", value = "1" } in the parameter group
findings.message: Redshift parameter group this: no require_ssl=1 parameter declared. The cluster accepts plaintext psql connections — query data and credentials cross the wire unencrypted.
remediation: Add `parameter { name = "require_ssl" value = "1" }` to the parameter group.
Confirm clients can connect with sslmode=require; older drivers may need an upgrade.
findings.suggestion: encrypted = true
findings.title: Redshift parameter group does not enforce SSL connections

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda consumer: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Errors metric

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

findings.expected: permissions_boundary is the maximum-permissions ceiling on a role. Without it, a permissive policy attached later can grant unintended privileges. Roles created by developers should ALWAYS have a boundary set.
findings.message: IAM role lambda: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
remediation: Define an aws_iam_policy that represents the maximum allowed permissions for this role's category (e.g. "developer-boundary"). Set permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn on the role.
findings.suggestion: permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn
findings.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: aws_budgets_budget with notification { threshold = 80 }
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly_alert" {
  name         = "monthly-spend-alert"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = "500"    # set to 120% of your expected monthly spend
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.billing_alert_email]
  }
}
findings.message: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_redshift_cluster) but no aws_budgets_budget. Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.
remediation: Add aws_budgets_budget { budget_type = "COST", limit_amount = "<monthly-limit>", time_unit = "MONTHLY" } with a notification block at 80% threshold.
Subscribe billing alerts to a team email or SNS topic so the alarm reaches someone with the ability to act.
findings.title: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function consumer: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function consumer: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: Lambdas behind API Gateway REST APIs should have timeout <= 29s (the API Gateway integration ceiling). Lambdas behind ALB or HTTP API can go up to 30 minutes.
findings.message: Lambda consumer: timeout = 30s, which exceeds the 29s API Gateway REST integration ceiling. If this Lambda is fronted by APIGW REST, the client will see a 504 at 29s while the Lambda keeps running, billing, and potentially producing side effects post-504. Cross-check with your aws_api_gateway_integration resources.
remediation: If fronted by APIGW REST: cap Lambda timeout at 28s, push long work to Step Functions Async or SQS. Return a 202 + correlation ID.
If fronted by ALB or HTTP API: ignore this finding — those have 30-min and 30s ceilings respectively.
findings.suggestion: timeout = 28
findings.title: Lambda integration timeout shape (timeout config sanity)

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda consumer: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda consumer: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: Lambdas in a VPC have a one-time cold start penalty (~1s) for ENI attachment, mitigated by Hyperplane ENI re-use post-2019 but still non-trivial. If the only reason this Lambda is in the VPC is to reach RDS/ElastiCache, consider RDS Proxy or moving the function to a public subnet path.
findings.message: Lambda consumer: declared with vpc_config (subnet_ids set). VPC Lambdas pay an ENI cold-start penalty. Worth verifying the VPC placement is for a real reason (private RDS/ElastiCache access) and not legacy 'we always put Lambdas in a VPC' inertia.
remediation: Confirm reason: RDS access? Use RDS Proxy + non-VPC Lambda. Internal service access? Confirm the VPC placement is current.
findings.suggestion: # Lambda in VPC behind REST API: add aws_api_gateway_vpc_link to avoid NAT Gateway egress charges
findings.title: API Gateway REST API with no VPC link AND backend Lambda is in a VPC

findings.expected: See remediation steps.
findings.message: Lambda event source mapping consumes from SQS, but the function's role appears to not grant sqs:ReceiveMessage / DeleteMessage. Mapping will create a permanent error backoff.
remediation: Attach an IAM policy granting sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage, sqs:GetQueueAttributes on the queue arn.
findings.title: Lambda event source mapping sqs → SQS, but consumer role missing sqs:ReceiveMessage

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: raw: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.message: Lambda function transform: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function transform: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.transform.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "transform_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "transform-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.transform.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda transform: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "transform_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "transform-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.transform.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda transform: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with expiration and abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rules
findings.message: S3 bucket (logs) has no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.
remediation: Add aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with an abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rule (7 days) to clean up abandoned uploads — these are invisible in the console but accumulate.
Add expiration rules for objects older than your retention window. For versioned buckets, add noncurrent_version_expiration (e.g. 30 days) to prevent version tombstones from accumulating.
findings.title: 1 S3 bucket without a lifecycle configuration

findings.expected: See remediation steps.
findings.message: Secret declared but no resource references it. Pays the per-secret monthly fee for nothing.
remediation: Schedule the secret for deletion (recovery_window_in_days ≥ 7).
findings.title: Secret redshift_master is unused

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.40" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.40.0 (patches only) or = 5.40.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 3.6" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 3.6.0 (patches only) or = 3.6.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: Debug/management ports restricted to bastion or corporate CIDRs (NOT 0.0.0.0/0)

remediation: Inspect aws_security_group.redshift and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.

findings.expected: A sibling aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation resource, or a rotation lambda attached
findings.message: Secrets Manager secret redshift: verify a rotation schedule is configured (aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation with rotation_rules.automatically_after_days <= 90).
remediation: Add an aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation referencing the secret, with automatically_after_days = 30 or 90
Implement the rotation Lambda (AWS publishes templates for RDS, DocumentDB, Redshift, and custom use cases)

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "transformer_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "transformer-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.transformer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda transformer: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "transformer_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "transformer_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.transformer_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue transformer_dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "transformer_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "transformer_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.transformer_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

findings.expected: permissions_boundary is the maximum-permissions ceiling on a role. Without it, a permissive policy attached later can grant unintended privileges. Roles created by developers should ALWAYS have a boundary set.
findings.message: IAM role transformer: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
remediation: Define an aws_iam_policy that represents the maximum allowed permissions for this role's category (e.g. "developer-boundary"). Set permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn on the role.
findings.suggestion: permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn
findings.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: landing: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: aws_budgets_budget with notification { threshold = 80 }
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly_alert" {
  name         = "monthly-spend-alert"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = "500"    # set to 120% of your expected monthly spend
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.billing_alert_email]
  }
}
findings.message: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_redshift_cluster) but no aws_budgets_budget. Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.
remediation: Add aws_budgets_budget { budget_type = "COST", limit_amount = "<monthly-limit>", time_unit = "MONTHLY" } with a notification block at 80% threshold.
Subscribe billing alerts to a team email or SNS topic so the alarm reaches someone with the ability to act.
findings.title: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

findings.expected: Lambdas behind API Gateway REST APIs should have timeout <= 29s (the API Gateway integration ceiling). Lambdas behind ALB or HTTP API can go up to 30 minutes.
findings.message: Lambda transformer: timeout = 300s, which exceeds the 29s API Gateway REST integration ceiling. If this Lambda is fronted by APIGW REST, the client will see a 504 at 29s while the Lambda keeps running, billing, and potentially producing side effects post-504. Cross-check with your aws_api_gateway_integration resources.
remediation: If fronted by APIGW REST: cap Lambda timeout at 28s, push long work to Step Functions Async or SQS. Return a 202 + correlation ID.
If fronted by ALB or HTTP API: ignore this finding — those have 30-min and 30s ceilings respectively.
findings.suggestion: timeout = 28
findings.title: Lambda integration timeout shape (timeout config sanity)

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy transformer: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Sid = "Logs", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"], Resource = "${aws_cloudwatch_log_group.transformer.arn}:*" }, { Sid = "ReadSourceCsv", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:GetObjectVersion"], Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.landing.arn}/*" }, { Sid = "ListSourceBucket", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["s3:ListBucket"], Resource = aws_s3_bucket.landing.arn }, { Sid = "UseKmsKey", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey", "kms:DescribeKey"], Resource = aws_kms_key.pipeline.arn }, { Sid = "ReadRedshiftSecret", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"], Resource = aws_secretsmanager_secret.redshift.arn }, { Sid = "RedshiftDataApi", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["redshift-data:ExecuteStatement", "redshift-data:BatchExecuteStatement", "redshift-data:DescribeStatement", "redshift-data:GetStatementResult"], Resource = "*", Condition = { StringEquals = { "aws:ResourceAccount" = data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id } } }, { Sid = "DeadLetterQueue", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["sqs:SendMessage"], Resource = aws_sqs_queue.transformer_dlq.arn }, { Sid = "VpcNetworking", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["ec2:CreateNetworkInterface", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces", "ec2:DeleteNetworkInterface"], Resource = "*" }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy redshift_copy: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Sid = "ReadLandingBucket", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:GetObjectVersion"], Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.landing.arn}/*" }, { Sid = "ListLandingBucket", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["s3:ListBucket"], Resource = aws_s3_bucket.landing.arn }, { Sid = "DecryptObjects", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:DescribeKey"], Resource = aws_kms_key.pipeline.arn }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: Lambdas in a VPC have a one-time cold start penalty (~1s) for ENI attachment, mitigated by Hyperplane ENI re-use post-2019 but still non-trivial. If the only reason this Lambda is in the VPC is to reach RDS/ElastiCache, consider RDS Proxy or moving the function to a public subnet path.
findings.message: Lambda transformer: declared with vpc_config (subnet_ids set). VPC Lambdas pay an ENI cold-start penalty. Worth verifying the VPC placement is for a real reason (private RDS/ElastiCache access) and not legacy 'we always put Lambdas in a VPC' inertia.
remediation: Confirm reason: RDS access? Use RDS Proxy + non-VPC Lambda. Internal service access? Confirm the VPC placement is current.
findings.suggestion: # Lambda in VPC behind REST API: add aws_api_gateway_vpc_link to avoid NAT Gateway egress charges
findings.title: API Gateway REST API with no VPC link AND backend Lambda is in a VPC

findings.expected: either parquet/ORC classification OR catalog-level SSE-KMS encryption configured
findings.message: Glue catalog table clickstream: classification=json + catalog has no SSE-KMS encryption. Metadata + sample-row exposure on a permissive principal becomes a data-leak vector.
remediation: Add or update an aws_glue_data_catalog_encryption_settings resource and set encryption_at_rest.catalog_encryption_mode = SSE-KMS with a customer-managed KMS key.
Migrate JSON-classified tables to Parquet where feasible (smaller blast radius on metadata leak + 5-10x query cost reduction).
findings.title: Glue catalog table is JSON-classified with no catalog encryption

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda processor: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Errors metric

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "lambda_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "lambda_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.lambda_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue lambda_dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "lambda_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "lambda_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.lambda_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: data: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: athena_results: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function processor: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function processor: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda processor: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda processor: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy lambda: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Sid = "ReadKinesis", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kinesis:GetRecords", "kinesis:GetShardIterator", "kinesis:DescribeStream", "kinesis:DescribeStreamSummary", "kinesis:ListShards"], Resource = aws_kinesis_stream.clickstream.arn }, { Sid = "WriteCuratedData", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["s3:PutObject"], Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.data.arn}/clickstream/*" }, { Sid = "SendToDlq", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["sqs:SendMessage"], Resource = aws_sqs_queue.lambda_dlq.arn }, { Sid = "UseCmk", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"], Resource = aws_kms_key.clickstream.arn }, { Sid = "WriteLogs", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents"], Resource = "${aws_cloudwatch_log_group.lambda.arn}:*" }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.40" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.40.0 (patches only) or = 5.40.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.processor and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.lambda has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.message: KMS key pipeline: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.
remediation: Set deletion_window_in_days = 30 (maximum)
findings.suggestion: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.title: KMS key deletion window should be the maximum (30 days)

findings.expected: dead_letter_config { target_arn = <SNS topic or SQS queue arn> }
findings.message: Lambda function this: no dead_letter_config. Async-invocation failures retry twice and disappear silently — no signal, no audit trail.
remediation: Create an SQS queue or SNS topic to receive failed invocations. Encrypt with a CMK.
Add `dead_letter_config { target_arn = ... }` to the function and grant the function role sqs:SendMessage / sns:Publish.
Wire an alarm on the DLQ depth so failed invocations don't sit unread.
findings.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

findings.message: items: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function this: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function this: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.this.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "this_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "this-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.this.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda this: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "this_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "this-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.this.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda this: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.40" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.40.0 (patches only) or = 5.40.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 2.4" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 2.4.0 (patches only) or = 2.4.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.this and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.read has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.write has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.delete has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: deletion_protection_enabled = true for production tables
findings.message: DynamoDB table todos: deletion_protection_enabled is false. An accidental terraform destroy or API call will permanently delete the table.
remediation: Set deletion_protection_enabled = true on production tables
findings.suggestion: deletion_protection_enabled = true
findings.title: DynamoDB table should have deletion protection enabled (for production)

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "api-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda api: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:

  metric_name         = "Errors"

  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}

context_suppressed.reason: aws_lambda_function.api is not invoked async-push (sync via API GW / Function URL, or polled-async via SQS / Kinesis event source mapping) — Lambda-level DLQ would never receive events. The invoker handles failures.
context_suppressed.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.lambda_exec has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: dead_letter_config { target_arn = <SNS topic or SQS queue arn> }
findings.message: Lambda function this: no dead_letter_config. Async-invocation failures retry twice and disappear silently — no signal, no audit trail.
remediation: Create an SQS queue or SNS topic to receive failed invocations. Encrypt with a CMK.
Add `dead_letter_config { target_arn = ... }` to the function and grant the function role sqs:SendMessage / sns:Publish.
Wire an alarm on the DLQ depth so failed invocations don't sit unread.
findings.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

findings.expected: permissions_boundary is the maximum-permissions ceiling on a role. Without it, a permissive policy attached later can grant unintended privileges. Roles created by developers should ALWAYS have a boundary set.
findings.message: IAM role lambda: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
remediation: Define an aws_iam_policy that represents the maximum allowed permissions for this role's category (e.g. "developer-boundary"). Set permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn on the role.
findings.suggestion: permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn
findings.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "api-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda api: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "api-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda api: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.40" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.40.0 (patches only) or = 5.40.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.api and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_lambda_function.api is not invoked async-push (sync via API GW / Function URL, or polled-async via SQS / Kinesis event source mapping) — Lambda-level DLQ would never receive events. The invoker handles failures.
context_suppressed.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

findings.expected: deletion_protection_enabled = true for production tables
findings.message: DynamoDB table items: deletion_protection_enabled is false. An accidental terraform destroy or API call will permanently delete the table.
remediation: Set deletion_protection_enabled = true on production tables
findings.suggestion: deletion_protection_enabled = true
findings.title: DynamoDB table should have deletion protection enabled (for production)

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "read_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "read-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.read.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda read: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "read_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "read-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.read.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Errors metric

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: items: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function read: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function write: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function read: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.read.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "lambda_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "lambda_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.lambda_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue lambda_dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "lambda_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "lambda_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.lambda_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

findings.expected: permissions_boundary is the maximum-permissions ceiling on a role. Without it, a permissive policy attached later can grant unintended privileges. Roles created by developers should ALWAYS have a boundary set.
findings.message: IAM role lambda: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
remediation: Define an aws_iam_policy that represents the maximum allowed permissions for this role's category (e.g. "developer-boundary"). Set permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn on the role.
findings.suggestion: permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn
findings.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda api: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy lambda_app: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Sid = "DynamoDBAccess", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["dynamodb:GetItem", "dynamodb:PutItem", "dynamodb:UpdateItem", "dynamodb:DeleteItem", "dynamodb:Query"], Resource = [aws_dynamodb_table.metadata.arn, "${aws_dynamodb_table.metadata.arn}/index/*"] }, { Sid = "S3UploadAccess", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"], Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.uploads.arn}/*" }, { Sid = "S3ListUploads", Effect = "Allow", Action = "s3:ListBucket", Resource = aws_s3_bucket.uploads.arn }, { Sid = "DLQAccess", Effect = "Allow", Action = "sqs:SendMessage", Resource = aws_sqs_queue.lambda_dlq.arn }, { Sid = "KMSAccess", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"], Resource = aws_kms_key.main.arn }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with expiration and abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rules
findings.message: S3 bucket (access_logs) has no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.
remediation: Add aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with an abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rule (7 days) to clean up abandoned uploads — these are invisible in the console but accumulate.
Add expiration rules for objects older than your retention window. For versioned buckets, add noncurrent_version_expiration (e.g. 30 days) to prevent version tombstones from accumulating.
findings.title: 1 S3 bucket without a lifecycle configuration

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)
