findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.40" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.40.0 (patches only) or = 5.40.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 3.6" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 3.6.0 (patches only) or = 3.6.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: Debug/management ports restricted to bastion or corporate CIDRs (NOT 0.0.0.0/0)
findings.message: Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks
remediation: Inspect aws_security_group.alb and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.

findings.expected: A sibling aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation resource, or a rotation lambda attached
findings.message: Secrets Manager secret db: verify a rotation schedule is configured (aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation with rotation_rules.automatically_after_days <= 90).
remediation: Add an aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation referencing the secret, with automatically_after_days = 30 or 90
Implement the rotation Lambda (AWS publishes templates for RDS, DocumentDB, Redshift, and custom use cases)
findings.title: Secrets Manager secret should have automatic rotation configured

findings.expected: configuration.execute_command_configuration with cloud_watch_log_group_name set
findings.message: ECS cluster main: execute-command configuration is missing — when operators run `aws ecs execute-command`, sessions are not logged. Investigations of insider misuse become unreliable.
remediation: Add a `configuration { execute_command_configuration { logging = "OVERRIDE" log_configuration { ... } } }` block to the cluster.
findings.title: ECS service does not enable execute-command logging

findings.expected: ALBs should have an alarm on UnHealthyHostCount per target group — fires when targets fail health checks. Without it, partial degradation (e.g. 1 of 3 hosts unhealthy) drifts unnoticed.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = {
    LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix
    TargetGroup  = aws_lb_target_group.main.arn_suffix
  }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: ALB main: no alarm references this load balancer. Target health is a load-balancer concern — without a paging hook, a target group running with 50% healthy hosts is silent until the last host fails.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/ApplicationELB, metric_name=UnHealthyHostCount, dimensions={LoadBalancer, TargetGroup}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = { LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: ALB has no CloudWatch alarm on UnHealthyHostCount

findings.expected: RDS instances should alarm on at least CPUUtilization (>= 80% sustained) and DatabaseConnections (close to instance class limit). Free disk space alarms come third.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "main-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: RDS main: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on CPUUtilization (>= 80% for 5 min) and DatabaseConnections (>= 80% of the instance class limit).
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "main-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: RDS instance has no CloudWatch alarm on CPU or DatabaseConnections

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: main: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: an access_logs { bucket = <s3 bucket>, enabled = true } block
findings.message: Load balancer main: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.
remediation: Add access_logs { bucket = aws_s3_bucket.lb_logs.id, enabled = true } and grant the regional ELB log-delivery account s3:PutObject on the bucket prefix.
findings.title: Load balancer has no access logging

findings.expected: aws_budgets_budget with notification { threshold = 80 }
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly_alert" {
  name         = "monthly-spend-alert"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = "500"    # set to 120% of your expected monthly spend
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.billing_alert_email]
  }
}
findings.message: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_db_instance, 1 aws_nat_gateway) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$33/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.
remediation: Add aws_budgets_budget { budget_type = "COST", limit_amount = "<monthly-limit>", time_unit = "MONTHLY" } with a notification block at 80% threshold.
Subscribe billing alerts to a team email or SNS topic so the alarm reaches someone with the ability to act.
findings.title: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

findings.expected: setting { name = "containerInsights", value = "enabled" }
findings.message: ECS cluster main: Container Insights is not enabled. CPU/memory/network metrics aren't aggregated; capacity-planning and incident triage rely on raw task-level metrics.
remediation: Add `setting { name = "containerInsights" value = "enabled" }` to the cluster.
findings.title: ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy ecs_execution_secrets: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Effect = "Allow", Action = ["secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"], Resource = [aws_secretsmanager_secret.db.arn] }, { Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt"], Resource = [aws_kms_key.main.arn] }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: An unattached EIP costs ~$3.60/month each — AWS bills for the static IP whether it's bound or not. EIPs allocated for future use or as DR placeholders should be tagged so the bill is auditable.
findings.message: EIP nat: no instance, network_interface, or associate_with_private_ip set. If nothing else binds this in another file, AWS charges for an unused static IP every month.
remediation: If actually needed: confirm the binding lives in another resource (aws_eip_association, aws_nat_gateway.allocation_id, aws_lb's subnet_mapping.allocation_id). Tag with Owner + Purpose either way.
If no longer needed: terraform destroy this EIP.
findings.suggestion: # Associate or release the unattached Elastic IP — unattached EIPs cost $0.005/hr (~$3.60/mo each)
findings.title: Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association

findings.expected: NAT gateways cost ~$32/month each plus $0.045/GB. Per-AZ HA NAT is right for prod with sustained traffic; for small accounts, a single shared NAT through a transit gateway or VPC peer is dramatically cheaper. Worth confirming intent.
findings.message: NAT Gateway main: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.
remediation: If dev/staging: collapse to a single shared NAT, route private subnets in other AZs through it. Accept the cross-AZ data charge as cheaper than per-AZ NAT.
findings.suggestion: # Use one shared NAT gateway per account ($0.045/hr) rather than per-AZ — upgrade to HA only when needed
findings.title: Multiple NAT gateways in a small account (one shared NAT is enough)

findings.expected: Lambdas / EC2 / ECS in this VPC calling secretsmanager:GetSecretValue route through NAT and pay per-GB egress. A com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager interface endpoint costs ~$7/AZ/month + drastically reduces NAT bytes.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secretsmanager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager (Interface type), associate with the VPC's subnets.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secrets_manager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

findings.expected: KMS calls dominate per-request cost on workloads that decrypt-on-each-read (Lambda + Secrets Manager + KMS Decrypt is the canonical pattern). VPC endpoint to KMS routes those calls without NAT egress.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
remediation: Add aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.kms.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

findings.expected: Container clusters pull images on every node bootstrap, every rolling deploy, every scale-out. ECR through NAT is one of the biggest single line-items on a busy ECS/EKS bill. The com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api + .ecr.dkr + .s3 endpoint trio kills it.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_dkr" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.dkr"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name      = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.s3"
  vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway"  # free
  route_table_ids   = aws_route_table.private[*].id
}
findings.message: Cluster main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
remediation: Add three endpoints: com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.s3 (Gateway). The S3 gateway endpoint is free.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: EKS / ECS cluster present but no ECR VPC endpoints

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: main: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
remediation: Add aws_inspector2_enabler { account_ids = [<this account>], resource_types = ["ECR", "EC2", "LAMBDA"] }.
findings.title: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

findings.expected: default_action { type = "redirect", redirect { protocol = "HTTPS", status_code = "HTTP_301" } }
findings.message: ALB listener http_forward: protocol is HTTP and default action is not a redirect to HTTPS. Client connections are accepted over plaintext — session tokens, credentials, and API responses are transmitted unencrypted.
remediation: Change default_action.type to "redirect" with redirect { port = "443", protocol = "HTTPS", status_code = "HTTP_301" }.
findings.title: ALB listener uses HTTP without a redirect to HTTPS

findings.expected: an aws_flow_log resource with vpc_id = aws_vpc.<this>.id and traffic_type = ALL
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.
remediation: Create an aws_flow_log resource with vpc_id = <vpc_id>, traffic_type = "ALL", log_destination pointing at S3 or CloudWatch logs.
findings.title: VPC has no associated flow logs

findings.expected: Internet-facing ALBs paired with Route53 records should have a companion Route53 health check so DNS can fail over to a backup endpoint when the LB is unhealthy.
findings.message: ALB app: internet-facing, no aws_route53_health_check references it. DNS keeps pointing at this LB even when it's serving 5xx — no upstream failover.
remediation: Add an aws_route53_health_check for the LB's DNS name + path. Wire into an aws_route53_record with failover routing.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_route53_health_check" "app" {
  fqdn              = aws_lb.app.dns_name
  port              = 443
  type              = "HTTPS"
  resource_path     = "/health"
  failure_threshold = 3
  request_interval  = 30
}
findings.title: Public ALB has no Route53 health check on its alias

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: main: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: an access_logs { bucket = <s3 bucket>, enabled = true } block
findings.message: Load balancer app: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.
remediation: Add access_logs { bucket = aws_s3_bucket.lb_logs.id, enabled = true } and grant the regional ELB log-delivery account s3:PutObject on the bucket prefix.
findings.title: Load balancer has no access logging

findings.expected: aws_budgets_budget with notification { threshold = 80 }
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly_alert" {
  name         = "monthly-spend-alert"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = "500"    # set to 120% of your expected monthly spend
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.billing_alert_email]
  }
}
findings.message: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_autoscaling_group, 1 aws_db_instance, 1 aws_nat_gateway) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$33/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.
remediation: Add aws_budgets_budget { budget_type = "COST", limit_amount = "<monthly-limit>", time_unit = "MONTHLY" } with a notification block at 80% threshold.
Subscribe billing alerts to a team email or SNS topic so the alarm reaches someone with the ability to act.
findings.title: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

findings.expected: An unattached EIP costs ~$3.60/month each — AWS bills for the static IP whether it's bound or not. EIPs allocated for future use or as DR placeholders should be tagged so the bill is auditable.
findings.message: EIP nat: no instance, network_interface, or associate_with_private_ip set. If nothing else binds this in another file, AWS charges for an unused static IP every month.
remediation: If actually needed: confirm the binding lives in another resource (aws_eip_association, aws_nat_gateway.allocation_id, aws_lb's subnet_mapping.allocation_id). Tag with Owner + Purpose either way.
If no longer needed: terraform destroy this EIP.
findings.suggestion: # Associate or release the unattached Elastic IP — unattached EIPs cost $0.005/hr (~$3.60/mo each)
findings.title: Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association

findings.expected: NAT gateways cost ~$32/month each plus $0.045/GB. Per-AZ HA NAT is right for prod with sustained traffic; for small accounts, a single shared NAT through a transit gateway or VPC peer is dramatically cheaper. Worth confirming intent.
findings.message: NAT Gateway main: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.
remediation: If dev/staging: collapse to a single shared NAT, route private subnets in other AZs through it. Accept the cross-AZ data charge as cheaper than per-AZ NAT.
findings.suggestion: # Use one shared NAT gateway per account ($0.045/hr) rather than per-AZ — upgrade to HA only when needed
findings.title: Multiple NAT gateways in a small account (one shared NAT is enough)

findings.expected: Lambdas / EC2 / ECS in this VPC calling secretsmanager:GetSecretValue route through NAT and pay per-GB egress. A com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager interface endpoint costs ~$7/AZ/month + drastically reduces NAT bytes.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secretsmanager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager (Interface type), associate with the VPC's subnets.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secrets_manager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

findings.expected: KMS calls dominate per-request cost on workloads that decrypt-on-each-read (Lambda + Secrets Manager + KMS Decrypt is the canonical pattern). VPC endpoint to KMS routes those calls without NAT egress.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
remediation: Add aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.kms.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: Debug/management ports restricted to bastion or corporate CIDRs (NOT 0.0.0.0/0)
findings.message: Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: ingress.cidr_blocks
remediation: Inspect aws_security_group.alb and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] Security group permits 0.0.0.0/0 on a known debug or management port

findings.expected: ASG instances cycle constantly. delete_on_termination=false leaves orphaned EBS volumes around forever, each billing $0.10/GB-month indefinitely. Only legitimate for stateful instances explicitly managed outside the ASG.
findings.message: EBS volume in an ASG launch template with delete_on_termination = false: cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_launch_template.app and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] EBS volume in an ASG launch template with delete_on_termination = false

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.app has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: Policy contains a Deny statement with condition Bool aws:SecureTransport false
findings.message: S3 bucket policy on alb_logs does not reference aws:SecureTransport — traffic can potentially be accepted over plain HTTP.
remediation: Attach an aws_s3_bucket_policy with an explicit Deny statement when aws:SecureTransport is false
findings.title: S3 bucket policy should deny non-TLS (HTTP) access

findings.expected: A sibling aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation resource, or a rotation lambda attached
findings.message: Secrets Manager secret db: verify a rotation schedule is configured (aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation with rotation_rules.automatically_after_days <= 90).
remediation: Add an aws_secretsmanager_secret_rotation referencing the secret, with automatically_after_days = 30 or 90
Implement the rotation Lambda (AWS publishes templates for RDS, DocumentDB, Redshift, and custom use cases)
findings.title: Secrets Manager secret should have automatic rotation configured

findings.expected: configuration.execute_command_configuration with cloud_watch_log_group_name set
findings.message: ECS cluster main: execute-command configuration is missing — when operators run `aws ecs execute-command`, sessions are not logged. Investigations of insider misuse become unreliable.
remediation: Add a `configuration { execute_command_configuration { logging = "OVERRIDE" log_configuration { ... } } }` block to the cluster.
findings.title: ECS service does not enable execute-command logging

findings.expected: ALBs should have an alarm on UnHealthyHostCount per target group — fires when targets fail health checks. Without it, partial degradation (e.g. 1 of 3 hosts unhealthy) drifts unnoticed.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = {
    LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix
    TargetGroup  = aws_lb_target_group.main.arn_suffix
  }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: ALB main: no alarm references this load balancer. Target health is a load-balancer concern — without a paging hook, a target group running with 50% healthy hosts is silent until the last host fails.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/ApplicationELB, metric_name=UnHealthyHostCount, dimensions={LoadBalancer, TargetGroup}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = { LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: ALB has no CloudWatch alarm on UnHealthyHostCount

findings.expected: RDS instances should alarm on at least CPUUtilization (>= 80% sustained) and DatabaseConnections (close to instance class limit). Free disk space alarms come third.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "main-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: RDS main: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on CPUUtilization (>= 80% for 5 min) and DatabaseConnections (>= 80% of the instance class limit).
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "main-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: RDS instance has no CloudWatch alarm on CPU or DatabaseConnections

findings.expected: setting { name = "containerInsights", value = "enabled" }
findings.message: ECS cluster main: Container Insights is not enabled. CPU/memory/network metrics aren't aggregated; capacity-planning and incident triage rely on raw task-level metrics.
remediation: Add `setting { name = "containerInsights" value = "enabled" }` to the cluster.
findings.title: ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy task_execution_secrets: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Effect = "Allow", Action = ["secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"], Resource = [aws_secretsmanager_secret.db.arn] }, { Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt"], Resource = [aws_kms_key.data.arn] }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: Container clusters pull images on every node bootstrap, every rolling deploy, every scale-out. ECR through NAT is one of the biggest single line-items on a busy ECS/EKS bill. The com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api + .ecr.dkr + .s3 endpoint trio kills it.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_dkr" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.dkr"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name      = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.s3"
  vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway"  # free
  route_table_ids   = aws_route_table.private[*].id
}
findings.message: Cluster main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
remediation: Add three endpoints: com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.s3 (Gateway). The S3 gateway endpoint is free.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: EKS / ECS cluster present but no ECR VPC endpoints

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: main: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
remediation: Add aws_inspector2_enabler { account_ids = [<this account>], resource_types = ["ECR", "EC2", "LAMBDA"] }.
findings.title: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

findings.expected: aws_vpc_endpoint { service_name = "com.amazonaws.<region>.s3", vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" }
findings.message: Plan includes aws_nat_gateway.main and 1 S3 bucket (alb_logs) but no aws_vpc_endpoint for S3. All S3 API calls from private subnets flow through the NAT gateway at $0.045/GB data-processing charge. An S3 Gateway endpoint is completely free and eliminates that cost entirely.
findings.observed: <no aws_vpc_endpoint with S3 service in plan>
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint resource with vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" targeting the S3 service in your region. Associate it with the route tables used by your private subnets.
Add a DynamoDB Gateway endpoint while you're there — same pattern, also free, also eliminates NAT data charges for DynamoDB.
findings.title: NAT Gateway routes S3 traffic — add a free S3 Gateway endpoint

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: an aws_flow_log resource with vpc_id = aws_vpc.<this>.id and traffic_type = ALL
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.

findings.expected: ALBs should have an alarm on UnHealthyHostCount per target group — fires when targets fail health checks. Without it, partial degradation (e.g. 1 of 3 hosts unhealthy) drifts unnoticed.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = {
    LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix
    TargetGroup  = aws_lb_target_group.main.arn_suffix
  }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: ALB main: no alarm references this load balancer. Target health is a load-balancer concern — without a paging hook, a target group running with 50% healthy hosts is silent until the last host fails.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/ApplicationELB, metric_name=UnHealthyHostCount, dimensions={LoadBalancer, TargetGroup}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = { LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: ALB has no CloudWatch alarm on UnHealthyHostCount

findings.expected: RDS instances should alarm on at least CPUUtilization (>= 80% sustained) and DatabaseConnections (close to instance class limit). Free disk space alarms come third.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "postgres_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "postgres-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.postgres.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: RDS postgres: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on CPUUtilization (>= 80% for 5 min) and DatabaseConnections (>= 80% of the instance class limit).
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "postgres_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "postgres-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.postgres.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: RDS instance has no CloudWatch alarm on CPU or DatabaseConnections

findings.expected: Internet-facing ALBs paired with Route53 records should have a companion Route53 health check so DNS can fail over to a backup endpoint when the LB is unhealthy.
findings.message: ALB main: internet-facing, no aws_route53_health_check references it. DNS keeps pointing at this LB even when it's serving 5xx — no upstream failover.
remediation: Add an aws_route53_health_check for the LB's DNS name + path. Wire into an aws_route53_record with failover routing.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_route53_health_check" "main" {
  fqdn              = aws_lb.main.dns_name
  port              = 443
  type              = "HTTPS"
  resource_path     = "/health"
  failure_threshold = 3
  request_interval  = 30
}
findings.title: Public ALB has no Route53 health check on its alias

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: alb_logs: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: postgres: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: an access_logs { bucket = <s3 bucket>, enabled = true } block
findings.message: Load balancer main: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.
remediation: Add access_logs { bucket = aws_s3_bucket.lb_logs.id, enabled = true } and grant the regional ELB log-delivery account s3:PutObject on the bucket prefix.
findings.title: Load balancer has no access logging

findings.expected: aws_budgets_budget with notification { threshold = 80 }
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly_alert" {
  name         = "monthly-spend-alert"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = "500"    # set to 120% of your expected monthly spend
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.billing_alert_email]
  }
}
findings.message: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_autoscaling_group, 1 aws_db_instance, 1 aws_nat_gateway) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$33/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.
remediation: Add aws_budgets_budget { budget_type = "COST", limit_amount = "<monthly-limit>", time_unit = "MONTHLY" } with a notification block at 80% threshold.
Subscribe billing alerts to a team email or SNS topic so the alarm reaches someone with the ability to act.
findings.title: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

findings.expected: performance_insights_enabled = true AND performance_insights_kms_key_id pointing at a CMK
findings.message: RDS instance postgres: Performance Insights is enabled but no performance_insights_kms_key_id is set. PI data (query plans, parameter values) is encrypted with the AWS-managed key — no CMK rotation, no per-customer audit boundary.
remediation: Set performance_insights_kms_key_id = <CMK arn>. PI metadata can sometimes contain query parameters / row samples; the same CMK as the cluster is appropriate.
findings.suggestion: performance_insights_kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.rds.arn
findings.title: RDS Performance Insights uses AWS-managed key (not CMK)

findings.expected: An unattached EIP costs ~$3.60/month each — AWS bills for the static IP whether it's bound or not. EIPs allocated for future use or as DR placeholders should be tagged so the bill is auditable.
findings.message: EIP nat: no instance, network_interface, or associate_with_private_ip set. If nothing else binds this in another file, AWS charges for an unused static IP every month.
remediation: If actually needed: confirm the binding lives in another resource (aws_eip_association, aws_nat_gateway.allocation_id, aws_lb's subnet_mapping.allocation_id). Tag with Owner + Purpose either way.
If no longer needed: terraform destroy this EIP.
findings.suggestion: # Associate or release the unattached Elastic IP — unattached EIPs cost $0.005/hr (~$3.60/mo each)
findings.title: Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association

findings.expected: NAT gateways cost ~$32/month each plus $0.045/GB. Per-AZ HA NAT is right for prod with sustained traffic; for small accounts, a single shared NAT through a transit gateway or VPC peer is dramatically cheaper. Worth confirming intent.
findings.message: NAT Gateway main: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.
remediation: If dev/staging: collapse to a single shared NAT, route private subnets in other AZs through it. Accept the cross-AZ data charge as cheaper than per-AZ NAT.
findings.suggestion: # Use one shared NAT gateway per account ($0.045/hr) rather than per-AZ — upgrade to HA only when needed
findings.title: Multiple NAT gateways in a small account (one shared NAT is enough)

findings.expected: Lambdas / EC2 / ECS in this VPC calling secretsmanager:GetSecretValue route through NAT and pay per-GB egress. A com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager interface endpoint costs ~$7/AZ/month + drastically reduces NAT bytes.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secretsmanager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager (Interface type), associate with the VPC's subnets.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secrets_manager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

findings.expected: KMS calls dominate per-request cost on workloads that decrypt-on-each-read (Lambda + Secrets Manager + KMS Decrypt is the canonical pattern). VPC endpoint to KMS routes those calls without NAT egress.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
remediation: Add aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.kms.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

findings.expected: aws_vpc_endpoint { service_name = "com.amazonaws.<region>.s3", vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" }
findings.message: Plan includes aws_nat_gateway.main and 1 S3 bucket (alb_logs) but no aws_vpc_endpoint for S3. All S3 API calls from private subnets flow through the NAT gateway at $0.045/GB data-processing charge. An S3 Gateway endpoint is completely free and eliminates that cost entirely.

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: alb_logs: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: aurora: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.expected: an access_logs { bucket = <s3 bucket>, enabled = true } block
findings.message: Load balancer main: access logging is not enabled. Request-level forensics (source IP, path, status, latency, backend) are unavailable after an incident — you cannot reconstruct who hit what.
remediation: Add access_logs { bucket = aws_s3_bucket.lb_logs.id, enabled = true } and grant the regional ELB log-delivery account s3:PutObject on the bucket prefix.
findings.title: Load balancer has no access logging

findings.expected: aws_budgets_budget with notification { threshold = 80 }
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly_alert" {
  name         = "monthly-spend-alert"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = "500"    # set to 120% of your expected monthly spend
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.billing_alert_email]
  }
}
findings.message: Plan includes hourly-billed resources (1 aws_nat_gateway, 1 aws_rds_cluster) but no aws_budgets_budget. Estimated monthly spend from priceable resources in this plan: ~$33/month (us-east-1 on-demand). Without a cost alarm, a forgotten instance, misconfigured autoscaling, or credential compromise can run up an unbounded bill before the team notices.
remediation: Add aws_budgets_budget { budget_type = "COST", limit_amount = "<monthly-limit>", time_unit = "MONTHLY" } with a notification block at 80% threshold.
Subscribe billing alerts to a team email or SNS topic so the alarm reaches someone with the ability to act.
findings.title: No AWS Budget alarm in the Terraform plan

findings.expected: setting { name = "containerInsights", value = "enabled" }
findings.message: ECS cluster main: Container Insights is not enabled. CPU/memory/network metrics aren't aggregated; capacity-planning and incident triage rely on raw task-level metrics.
remediation: Add `setting { name = "containerInsights" value = "enabled" }` to the cluster.
findings.title: ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy ecs_execution_secrets: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Effect = "Allow", Action = ["secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"], Resource = aws_secretsmanager_secret.db.arn }, { Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt"], Resource = aws_kms_key.data.arn }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: An unattached EIP costs ~$3.60/month each — AWS bills for the static IP whether it's bound or not. EIPs allocated for future use or as DR placeholders should be tagged so the bill is auditable.
findings.message: EIP nat: no instance, network_interface, or associate_with_private_ip set. If nothing else binds this in another file, AWS charges for an unused static IP every month.
remediation: If actually needed: confirm the binding lives in another resource (aws_eip_association, aws_nat_gateway.allocation_id, aws_lb's subnet_mapping.allocation_id). Tag with Owner + Purpose either way.
If no longer needed: terraform destroy this EIP.
findings.suggestion: # Associate or release the unattached Elastic IP — unattached EIPs cost $0.005/hr (~$3.60/mo each)
findings.title: Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association

findings.expected: NAT gateways cost ~$32/month each plus $0.045/GB. Per-AZ HA NAT is right for prod with sustained traffic; for small accounts, a single shared NAT through a transit gateway or VPC peer is dramatically cheaper. Worth confirming intent.
findings.message: NAT Gateway main: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.
remediation: If dev/staging: collapse to a single shared NAT, route private subnets in other AZs through it. Accept the cross-AZ data charge as cheaper than per-AZ NAT.
findings.suggestion: # Use one shared NAT gateway per account ($0.045/hr) rather than per-AZ — upgrade to HA only when needed
findings.title: Multiple NAT gateways in a small account (one shared NAT is enough)

findings.expected: Lambdas / EC2 / ECS in this VPC calling secretsmanager:GetSecretValue route through NAT and pay per-GB egress. A com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager interface endpoint costs ~$7/AZ/month + drastically reduces NAT bytes.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secretsmanager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager (Interface type), associate with the VPC's subnets.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secrets_manager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

findings.expected: KMS calls dominate per-request cost on workloads that decrypt-on-each-read (Lambda + Secrets Manager + KMS Decrypt is the canonical pattern). VPC endpoint to KMS routes those calls without NAT egress.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
remediation: Add aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.kms.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

findings.expected: Container clusters pull images on every node bootstrap, every rolling deploy, every scale-out. ECR through NAT is one of the biggest single line-items on a busy ECS/EKS bill. The com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api + .ecr.dkr + .s3 endpoint trio kills it.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_dkr" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.dkr"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name      = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.s3"
  vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway"  # free
  route_table_ids   = aws_route_table.private[*].id
}
findings.message: Cluster main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
remediation: Add three endpoints: com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.s3 (Gateway). The S3 gateway endpoint is free.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: EKS / ECS cluster present but no ECR VPC endpoints

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: main: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
remediation: Add aws_inspector2_enabler { account_ids = [<this account>], resource_types = ["ECR", "EC2", "LAMBDA"] }.
findings.title: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

findings.expected: aws_vpc_endpoint { service_name = "com.amazonaws.<region>.s3", vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" }
findings.message: Plan includes aws_nat_gateway.main and 1 S3 bucket (alb_logs) but no aws_vpc_endpoint for S3. All S3 API calls from private subnets flow through the NAT gateway at $0.045/GB data-processing charge. An S3 Gateway endpoint is completely free and eliminates that cost entirely.
findings.observed: <no aws_vpc_endpoint with S3 service in plan>
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint resource with vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" targeting the S3 service in your region. Associate it with the route tables used by your private subnets.
Add a DynamoDB Gateway endpoint while you're there — same pattern, also free, also eliminates NAT data charges for DynamoDB.
findings.title: NAT Gateway routes S3 traffic — add a free S3 Gateway endpoint

findings.expected: See remediation steps.
findings.message: An aurora reader / cluster instance lives in a subnet whose route table has 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW. The cluster itself is `publicly_accessible = false`, but a misconfigured network appliance / NAT alternative could route inbound traffic toward it.
findings.observed: aws_rds_cluster.aurora subnet group: "aws_db_subnet_group.aurora.name"; Route tables with IGW route: ["aws_route_table.public"]
remediation: Move the cluster's subnet to a route table without an IGW route. Verify outbound NAT through a NAT Gateway only.
findings.title: Aurora cluster aurora reachable through a route table that has an IGW route

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 3.5" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 3.5.0 (patches only) or = 3.5.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: an aws_flow_log resource with vpc_id = aws_vpc.<this>.id and traffic_type = ALL
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_flow_log resource references this VPC. Network-level forensics (lateral movement, exfiltration) are unreliable post-incident.

findings.expected: ALBs should have an alarm on UnHealthyHostCount per target group — fires when targets fail health checks. Without it, partial degradation (e.g. 1 of 3 hosts unhealthy) drifts unnoticed.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = {
    LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix
    TargetGroup  = aws_lb_target_group.main.arn_suffix
  }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: ALB main: no alarm references this load balancer. Target health is a load-balancer concern — without a paging hook, a target group running with 50% healthy hosts is silent until the last host fails.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/ApplicationELB, metric_name=UnHealthyHostCount, dimensions={LoadBalancer, TargetGroup}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_unhealthy" {
  alarm_name          = "main-unhealthy-hosts"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  metric_name         = "UnHealthyHostCount"
  dimensions          = { LoadBalancer = aws_lb.main.arn_suffix }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: ALB has no CloudWatch alarm on UnHealthyHostCount

findings.expected: RDS instances should alarm on at least CPUUtilization (>= 80% sustained) and DatabaseConnections (close to instance class limit). Free disk space alarms come third.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "main-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: RDS main: no alarms reference this instance. CPU saturation and connection-pool exhaustion are the two failure modes engineers don't catch until customers complain. Both have CloudWatch metrics; neither has a configured threshold here.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on CPUUtilization (>= 80% for 5 min) and DatabaseConnections (>= 80% of the instance class limit).
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "main_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "main-cpu-high"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  dimensions          = { DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id }
  statistic           = "Average"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  datapoints_to_alarm = 2
  threshold           = 80
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: RDS instance has no CloudWatch alarm on CPU or DatabaseConnections

findings.expected: Internet-facing ALBs paired with Route53 records should have a companion Route53 health check so DNS can fail over to a backup endpoint when the LB is unhealthy.
findings.message: ALB main: internet-facing, no aws_route53_health_check references it. DNS keeps pointing at this LB even when it's serving 5xx — no upstream failover.
remediation: Add an aws_route53_health_check for the LB's DNS name + path. Wire into an aws_route53_record with failover routing.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_route53_health_check" "main" {
  fqdn              = aws_lb.main.dns_name
  port              = 443
  type              = "HTTPS"
  resource_path     = "/health"
  failure_threshold = 3
  request_interval  = 30
}
findings.title: Public ALB has no Route53 health check on its alias

remediation: If actually needed: confirm the binding lives in another resource (aws_eip_association, aws_nat_gateway.allocation_id, aws_lb's subnet_mapping.allocation_id). Tag with Owner + Purpose either way.
If no longer needed: terraform destroy this EIP.
findings.suggestion: # Associate or release the unattached Elastic IP — unattached EIPs cost $0.005/hr (~$3.60/mo each)
findings.title: Elastic IP declared without instance / NAT / NLB association

findings.expected: NAT gateways cost ~$32/month each plus $0.045/GB. Per-AZ HA NAT is right for prod with sustained traffic; for small accounts, a single shared NAT through a transit gateway or VPC peer is dramatically cheaper. Worth confirming intent.
findings.message: NAT Gateway main: declared. If your plan has multiple of these in the same VPC (one per AZ), the redundancy costs $32/month per extra AZ — fine for prod with steady traffic, but commonly accidental in dev/staging.
remediation: If dev/staging: collapse to a single shared NAT, route private subnets in other AZs through it. Accept the cross-AZ data charge as cheaper than per-AZ NAT.
findings.suggestion: # Use one shared NAT gateway per account ($0.045/hr) rather than per-AZ — upgrade to HA only when needed
findings.title: Multiple NAT gateways in a small account (one shared NAT is enough)

findings.expected: Lambdas / EC2 / ECS in this VPC calling secretsmanager:GetSecretValue route through NAT and pay per-GB egress. A com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager interface endpoint costs ~$7/AZ/month + drastically reduces NAT bytes.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secretsmanager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared. Secrets Manager calls from in-VPC workloads traverse NAT. At ~$0.045/GB through NAT, even modest secret-fetching patterns pay back the endpoint cost in days.
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.secretsmanager (Interface type), associate with the VPC's subnets.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "secrets_manager" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.secretsmanager"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (Lambda → Secrets traffic via NAT)

findings.expected: KMS calls dominate per-request cost on workloads that decrypt-on-each-read (Lambda + Secrets Manager + KMS Decrypt is the canonical pattern). VPC endpoint to KMS routes those calls without NAT egress.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.message: VPC main: no aws_vpc_endpoint for KMS. Decrypt calls leave the VPC via NAT — for high-volume workloads this adds up faster than the endpoint cost.
remediation: Add aws_vpc_endpoint for com.amazonaws.<region>.kms.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "kms" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.kms"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoints.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: VPC has no KMS VPC endpoint

findings.expected: aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with expiration and abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rules
findings.message: S3 bucket (alb_logs) has no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.
remediation: Add aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with an abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rule (7 days) to clean up abandoned uploads — these are invisible in the console but accumulate.
Add expiration rules for objects older than your retention window. For versioned buckets, add noncurrent_version_expiration (e.g. 30 days) to prevent version tombstones from accumulating.
findings.title: 1 S3 bucket without a lifecycle configuration

findings.expected: aws_vpc_endpoint { service_name = "com.amazonaws.<region>.s3", vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" }
findings.message: Plan includes aws_nat_gateway.main and 1 S3 bucket (alb_logs) but no aws_vpc_endpoint for S3. All S3 API calls from private subnets flow through the NAT gateway at $0.045/GB data-processing charge. An S3 Gateway endpoint is completely free and eliminates that cost entirely.
findings.observed: <no aws_vpc_endpoint with S3 service in plan>
remediation: Add an aws_vpc_endpoint resource with vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway" targeting the S3 service in your region. Associate it with the route tables used by your private subnets.
Add a DynamoDB Gateway endpoint while you're there — same pattern, also free, also eliminates NAT data charges for DynamoDB.
findings.title: NAT Gateway routes S3 traffic — add a free S3 Gateway endpoint

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 3.5" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 3.5.0 (patches only) or = 3.5.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.web has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

findings.expected: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.message: KMS key jobs: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.
remediation: Set deletion_window_in_days = 30 (maximum)
findings.suggestion: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.title: KMS key deletion window should be the maximum (30 days)

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda processor: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Errors metric

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "jobs_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "jobs_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.jobs_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue jobs_dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "jobs_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "jobs_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"

}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage
