findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function processor: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function processor: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda processor: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "processor_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "processor-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.processor.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda processor: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: See remediation steps.
findings.message: Lambda event source mapping consumes from SQS, but the function's role appears to not grant sqs:ReceiveMessage / DeleteMessage. Mapping will create a permanent error backoff.
remediation: Attach an IAM policy granting sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage, sqs:GetQueueAttributes on the queue arn.
findings.title: Lambda event source mapping jobs → SQS, but consumer role missing sqs:ReceiveMessage

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: default_tags { tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } }
findings.message: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
remediation: Add default_tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } to the provider block. New resources inherit automatically.
findings.title: AWS provider block has no default_tags

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.

remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.processor and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

findings.message: ECS cluster this: execute-command configuration is missing — when operators run `aws ecs execute-command`, sessions are not logged. Investigations of insider misuse become unreliable.
remediation: Add a `configuration { execute_command_configuration { logging = "OVERRIDE" log_configuration { ... } } }` block to the cluster.
findings.title: ECS service does not enable execute-command logging

findings.expected: setting { name = "containerInsights", value = "enabled" }
findings.message: ECS cluster this: Container Insights is not enabled. CPU/memory/network metrics aren't aggregated; capacity-planning and incident triage rely on raw task-level metrics.

findings.title: ECS cluster does not enable Container Insights

findings.expected: Container clusters pull images on every node bootstrap, every rolling deploy, every scale-out. ECR through NAT is one of the biggest single line-items on a busy ECS/EKS bill. The com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api + .ecr.dkr + .s3 endpoint trio kills it.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_dkr" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.dkr"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  security_group_ids  = [aws_security_group.vpc_endpoint.id]
  private_dns_enabled = true
}

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "s3" {
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name      = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.s3"
  vpc_endpoint_type = "Gateway"  # free
  route_table_ids   = aws_route_table.private[*].id
}
findings.message: Cluster this: no aws_vpc_endpoint declared for ECR or S3. Container image pulls + Docker layer downloads flow through NAT — common single-biggest contributor to NAT data-processing charges.
remediation: Add three endpoints: com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr (Interface), com.amazonaws.<region>.s3 (Gateway). The S3 gateway endpoint is free.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_vpc_endpoint" "ecr_api" {
  vpc_id              = aws_vpc.main.id
  service_name        = "com.amazonaws.${data.aws_region.current.name}.ecr.api"
  vpc_endpoint_type   = "Interface"
  subnet_ids          = var.private_subnet_ids
  private_dns_enabled = true
}
findings.title: EKS / ECS cluster present but no ECR VPC endpoints

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: this: no aws_inspector2_enabler in the plan. Container / runtime / EC2 CVEs aren't surfaced — the team is doing vulnerability management manually or not at all.
remediation: Add aws_inspector2_enabler { account_ids = [<this account>], resource_types = ["ECR", "EC2", "LAMBDA"] }.
findings.title: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: default_tags { tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } }
findings.message: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
remediation: Add default_tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } to the provider block. New resources inherit automatically.
findings.title: AWS provider block has no default_tags

findings.expected: Debug/management ports restricted to bastion or corporate CIDRs (NOT 0.0.0.0/0)

remediation: Inspect aws_security_group.worker and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.

findings.expected: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.message: KMS key this: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.
remediation: Set deletion_window_in_days = 30 (maximum)
findings.suggestion: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.title: KMS key deletion window should be the maximum (30 days)

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "this_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "this-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.this.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda this: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "dlq_age" {

  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"

}

findings.message: Lambda function this: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.

findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function this: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.this.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "this_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "this-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.this.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda this: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.

  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.this.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda this: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: permissions_boundary is the maximum-permissions ceiling on a role. Without it, a permissive policy attached later can grant unintended privileges. Roles created by developers should ALWAYS have a boundary set.
findings.message: IAM role lambda: permissions_boundary unset. Future policy attachments can grant unlimited privilege — the role's effective permissions = union of all attached policies, no ceiling.
remediation: Define an aws_iam_policy that represents the maximum allowed permissions for this role's category (e.g. "developer-boundary"). Set permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn on the role.
findings.suggestion: permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.developer_boundary.arn
findings.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary

remediation: Attach an IAM policy granting sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage, sqs:GetQueueAttributes on the queue arn.
findings.title: Lambda event source mapping email → SQS, but consumer role missing sqs:ReceiveMessage

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 2.4" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 2.4.0 (patches only) or = 2.4.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: default_tags { tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } }
findings.message: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
remediation: Add default_tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } to the provider block. New resources inherit automatically.
findings.title: AWS provider block has no default_tags

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.email_worker and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_lambda_function.email_worker is not invoked async-push (sync via API GW / Function URL, or polled-async via SQS / Kinesis event source mapping) — Lambda-level DLQ would never receive events. The invoker handles failures.
context_suppressed.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

findings.expected: Every Lambda should have an alarm on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, dimensioned by FunctionName, with a non-zero threshold and a route to a paging destination.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda consumer: no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm references this function. Errors land in CloudWatch but nobody is paged. The next time this Lambda starts throwing 500s, the on-call learns about it from a customer ticket.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/Lambda, metric_name=Errors, dimensions={FunctionName=<this>}, threshold=1, evaluation_periods=2, statistic=Sum, alarm_actions=[<sns topic that pages>].
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 60
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Errors metric

findings.expected: SQS consumers can get stuck (deployment failure, poison message, dependency outage). ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage rising linearly is the canonical signal — alarm fires when consumer is dead.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "jobs_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "jobs_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.jobs_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300  # 5 minutes
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.message: SQS queue jobs_dlq: no alarms reference this queue. Consumer death is invisible from the queue side until messages back up. ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage exceeding a few minutes is unambiguous evidence the consumer stopped processing.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm: namespace=AWS/SQS, metric_name=ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, dimensions={QueueName=<this>}, threshold=300 (5 min), evaluation_periods=2.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "jobs_dlq_age" {
  alarm_name          = "jobs_dlq-message-age"
  namespace           = "AWS/SQS"
  metric_name         = "ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage"
  dimensions          = { QueueName = aws_sqs_queue.jobs_dlq.name }
  statistic           = "Maximum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 300
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}
findings.title: SQS queue has no alarm on ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage

findings.expected: code_signing_config_arn pointing at an aws_lambda_code_signing_config with TRUSTED publishers
findings.message: Lambda function consumer: code_signing_config_arn is not set. Any artifact pushed to the function passes — there's no signing-publisher gate.
remediation: Create an aws_signer_signing_profile and an aws_lambda_code_signing_config that references it (with allowed publishers + an UntrustedArtifactOnDeployment = Enforce policy).
Set code_signing_config_arn on each function consuming this signer.
findings.suggestion: code_signing_config_arn = aws_lambda_code_signing_config.main.arn
findings.title: Lambda function does not enforce code signing

findings.expected: an aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource named /aws/lambda/<function_name> with KMS encryption + retention
findings.message: Lambda function consumer: no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group declared. Lambda will auto-create one with default retention (never expires) and AES256 encryption — no CMK, no controlled retention.
remediation: Add an aws_cloudwatch_log_group with name = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name}", retention_in_days = 90, kms_key_id = <CMK arn>.
findings.title: Lambda function has no explicit CloudWatch log group

findings.expected: An alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles fires when this Lambda hits its reserved concurrency cap OR the account-level cap. Without it, traffic loss during a concurrency spike is invisible.
findings.fix_snippet:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.message: Lambda consumer: no alarm on Throttles. When this function gets throttled (own cap or account cap), invocations fail silently from the caller's perspective — neither logs nor metrics-as-configured surface it as a paging signal.
remediation: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm on AWS/Lambda Throttles for this function. Threshold can be modest (1 over 5 minutes) — Throttles should normally be zero.
findings.suggestion:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "consumer_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "consumer-throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  dimensions          = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.consumer.function_name }
  statistic           = "Sum"
  period              = 300
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  datapoints_to_alarm = 1
  threshold           = 1
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
  treat_missing_data  = "notBreaching"
}
findings.title: Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarm on Throttles metric

findings.expected: Most teams have at least one CloudWatch dashboard per service surface — Lambda invocations + errors + p99 + concurrency on one wall. Plans with Lambda but zero dashboards usually rely on hand-rolled CLI commands during incidents.
findings.message: Lambda consumer: this plan declares no aws_cloudwatch_dashboard. If incidents are debugged by typing CloudWatch URLs into the AWS console one tab at a time, the next on-call engineer will spend their first 5 minutes rebuilding mental state someone else already had.
remediation: Add one aws_cloudwatch_dashboard with widgets for Errors, Duration p99, Invocations, ConcurrentExecutions per Lambda. ~50 lines of HCL for a 5-Lambda system.
findings.title: Lambda exists but no CloudWatch dashboard declared in the same plan

findings.expected: Highly-privileged actions (iam:PassRole, sts:AssumeRole, kms:Decrypt, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) should have a Condition block — bounding by aws:SourceVpce, aws:PrincipalTag, kms:ViaService, etc. — even when scoped by Resource. Bare grants are easier to abuse via a compromised caller.
findings.message: IAM policy consumer_sqs: grants a high-privilege action with no Condition block. Compromise of any principal holding this policy gives unconstrained use of that action.
findings.observed: jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17", Statement = [{ Sid = "ConsumeJobs", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["sqs:ReceiveMessage", "sqs:DeleteMessage", "sqs:GetQueueAttributes", "sqs:ChangeMessageVisibility"], Resource = aws_sqs_queue.jobs.arn }, { Sid = "DecryptMessages", Effect = "Allow", Action = ["kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"], Resource = aws_kms_key.jobs.arn }] })
remediation: Add a Condition appropriate to the action: kms:ViaService for KMS, aws:SourceVpce for Secrets Manager, aws:PrincipalTag for PassRole.
findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

remediation: Add default_tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } to the provider block. New resources inherit automatically.

findings.expected: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.message: KMS key this: deletion_window_in_days is less than the maximum of 30. If scheduled for deletion, there is less time to cancel and recover data encrypted with this key.
remediation: Set deletion_window_in_days = 30 (maximum)
findings.suggestion: deletion_window_in_days = 30
findings.title: KMS key deletion window should be the maximum (30 days)

findings.expected: Stateful resources (databases, user pools, content buckets) should have lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental terraform destroy on a production database is a career-defining event.
findings.message: uploads: no lifecycle.prevent_destroy block. A terraform destroy or refactor that removes this resource address will delete it without a guardrail.
remediation: Add lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } to the resource. To actually destroy, you flip it to false in a separate apply.
findings.title: Stateful resource missing lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }

findings.title: IAM policy grants privileged action with no Condition block

findings.expected: aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with expiration and abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rules
findings.message: S3 bucket (output) has no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration. Without lifecycle rules, objects accumulate forever: old versions from versioning, abandoned multipart uploads, and stale data all bill at $0.023/GB-month with no bound.
remediation: Add aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration with an abort_incomplete_multipart_upload rule (7 days) to clean up abandoned uploads — these are invisible in the console but accumulate.
Add expiration rules for objects older than your retention window. For versioned buckets, add noncurrent_version_expiration (e.g. 30 days) to prevent version tombstones from accumulating.
findings.title: 1 S3 bucket without a lifecycle configuration

findings.message: Terraform required_providers: version = "~> 5.0" allows minor upgrades. Minor bumps in the AWS provider regularly introduce breaking changes.
remediation: Pin to ~> 5.0.0 (patches only) or = 5.0.0 for exact reproducibility. Commit the terraform .lock.hcl to freeze the hash.
findings.title: Provider version uses too-loose constraint (~> N or pinned to major only)

findings.expected: default_tags { tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } }
findings.message: AWS provider: no default_tags block. Every resource has to tag itself for Cost Explorer — teams forget on a third of them.
remediation: Add default_tags = { Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy = "terraform" } to the provider block. New resources inherit automatically.
findings.title: AWS provider block has no default_tags

findings.expected: AWS Inspector v2 continuously scans ECR images, EC2 instances, and Lambda functions for OS / language-runtime CVEs. Free tier covers many small accounts; paid is cheap relative to the alternative of building it yourself.
findings.message: No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning): cannot verify — required attribute(s) missing from source: name
remediation: Inspect aws_lambda_function.resizer and confirm the missing attribute(s) are set correctly. If managed outside Terraform (console/CLI), export and re-import into IaC.
findings.title: [Needs Review] No aws_inspector2_enabler declared (no vulnerability scanning)

context_suppressed.reason: aws_lambda_function.resizer is not invoked async-push (sync via API GW / Function URL, or polled-async via SQS / Kinesis event source mapping) — Lambda-level DLQ would never receive events. The invoker handles failures.
context_suppressed.title: Lambda function has no dead-letter queue configured

context_suppressed.reason: aws_iam_role.lambda has no parsed policy grants that are exploitably dangerous (Allow + Resource:"*" + no Condition) — a permissions_boundary would add no meaningful ceiling to this role's current permissions.
context_suppressed.title: IAM role has no permissions_boundary
